title: Independent and Joint Prospective Associations of Screen Time and Sleep Disturbance with Body Mass Index and Waist Circumference Among U.S. Adolescents.
abstract: Background: We examined the independent and joint prospective associations of screen time, sleep disturbance, and sleep duration with body mass index (BMI) and waist circumference (WC) stratified by sex. Methods: Data are from 7445 participants (47.3% females) aged 9-10 years at baseline (2016-2018) in the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study. Missing data were imputed, and weighted multivariable linear regression models estimated the independent and joint effects of screen time and sleep disturbance or sleep duration on BMI and WC after 2 years. Joint associations grouped participants by combinations of screen time and sleep, with low screen time and sufficient sleep or no sleep disturbance as the reference groups. Results: Screen time was significantly associated with higher BMI (B = 0.32; 95% CI: 0.19, 0.45; p < 0.001) and WC (B = 0.40; 95% CI: 0.23, 0.56; p < 0.001). Sleep disturbance was associated with higher BMI (B = 1.23; 95% CI: 0.14, 2.33; p 0.026) in males. Insufficient sleep (<9 hours) (B = 1.30; 95% CI: 0.53, 2.07; p < 0.001) was associated with higher BMI. Medium screen time without sleep disturbance or insufficient sleep, and medium and high screen time with either sleep problem were associated with higher BMI and WC. Low screen time with insufficient sleep and high screen time with sufficient sleep were associated with higher BMI. Associations were generally stronger among males, particularly with high screen time. Conclusion: Screen time and insufficient sleep were independently and jointly associated with higher BMI and WC 2 years later, especially when both were present.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data: 7445 adolescents aged 9-10 years from Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study at baseline (2016-2018)

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time measured as composite across multiple modalities (TV, video games, social media, video chatting, etc.) without separately extractable smartphone or social media-specific coefficient. Results report only aggregate screen time associations. Violates FT-IC2 requirement.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Childhood Obesity (Print), October 2025

C1.FT_EC7_reason: FT-IC2 NOT_MET; does not reach criteria check for FT-EC7

FT_IC1.evidence: Paper explicitly analyzes U.S. ABCD Study data, n=7,445 adolescents ages 9-10 at baseline (2016-2018) with 2-year follow-up data. Text states 'Data are from 7445 participants...in the longitudinal Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study.'

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time measured as continuous variable (hours/day) and categorized (0-4, 4-8, ≥8 hours/day). Also measured by type (TV, movies, video games, socializing, texting, video chatting, social media). Enters all primary analyses.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple screen-to-non-screen linear regression tests: screen time (continuous) with BMI (β=0.32, 95%CI:0.19,0.45, p<.001), screen time with waist circumference (β=0.40, 95%CI:0.23,0.56, p<.001). Joint associations of screen time + sleep disturbance with BMI and WC reported in Tables 2-3.

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric coefficients reported throughout: β=0.32 (p<.001) for screen time→BMI overall; β=1.69 (95%CI:0.82,2.55) for 4-8 hrs vs reference; β=2.76 (95%CI:1.62,3.90) for 4-8 hrs medium screen vs low screen→WC.

FT_IC5.evidence: Prospective longitudinal cohort design analyzing independent and joint associations of screen time and sleep with BMI/WC at 2-year follow-up. Original empirical analysis with weighted multivariate linear regression models.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed journal article published in Childhood Obesity (October 2025), DOI: 10.1089/chi.2025.0022.

title: Preliminary findings on caffeine intake, screen time, social factors, and psychological well-being: their impact on chronotype and sleep health in Hispanic adolescents.
journal: Sleep advances : a journal of the Sleep Research Society
abstract: This study examined the relationships between caffeine intake, screen time, and chronotype/sleep outcomes in adolescents, with a focus on differences between Hispanic and non-Hispanic groups and the influence of peer network health, school environment, and psychological factors, including perceived stress, depression, and anxiety. Data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) study were analyzed using t-tests and structural equation modeling (SEM) to assess behavioral, social, and psychological predictors of chronotype, social jet lag, and weekday sleep duration, incorporating demographic covariates. Hispanic adolescents exhibited a later chronotype (Cohen's dâ=â0.42), greater social jet lag (Cohen's dâ=â0.38), and shorter weekday sleep duration (Cohen's dâ=â-0.12) compared to non-Hispanic peers. They also reported higher caffeine intake (Cohen's dâ=â0.22), though caffeine was not significantly associated with sleep outcomes. Screen time was more prevalent among Hispanic adolescents, particularly on weekday evenings (Cohen's dâ=â0.27) and weekend evenings (Cohen's dâ=â0.35), and was strongly associated with later chronotype and greater social jet lag. Higher perceived stress was linked to later chronotype and greater social jet lag, while depressive symptoms were associated with earlier chronotype and lower social jet lag. The SEM model explained 12.9% of variance in chronotype, 10.5% in social jet lag, and 6.2% in weekday sleep duration. These findings highlight disparities in adolescent sleep health but should be interpreted cautiously due to methodological limitations, including low caffeine use and assessment timing variability. Targeted interventions addressing screen time, peer relationships, and stress may improve sleep, while longitudinal research is needed to clarify causality.

FT_IC1.evidence: Abstract states ABCD study data but no PDF available; cannot confirm U.S. ABCD vs. Dutch ABCD or other cohort from abstract alone

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time measured: weekday evenings (Cohen's d=0.27) and weekend evenings (Cohen's d=0.35); reported as specific outcome variable associated with chronotype and sleep

FT_IC3.evidence: SEM and t-tests: screen time associated with chronotype, social jet lag, and weekday sleep duration; Cohen's d effect sizes reported

FT_IC4.evidence: Cohen's d values reported for screen time associations (weekday d=0.27, weekend d=0.35); SEM model variance explained (12.9% chronotype, 10.5% social jet lag, 6.2% sleep duration)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: cross-sectional and SEM examining behavioral and psychological predictors of sleep outcomes in Hispanic vs. non-Hispanic adolescents

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Sleep Advances (2025)

C1.FT_EC7_reason: Paper includes individual psychological predictors (perceived stress, depression, anxiety) as independent variables alongside demographics, predicting sleep outcomes. Screen time is one of multiple IVs, not the sole dependent variable. Does not meet FT-EC7 criteria.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data as stated in abstract: 'Data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) study were analyzed.'

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper measures screen time as a specific variable, including weekday evenings and weekend evenings with separate measurements. Screen time is measured and reported with platform indicators (e.g., 'Screen time was more prevalent among Hispanic adolescents, particularly on weekday evenings').

FT_IC3.evidence: SEM analyses conducted examining associations between screen time and chronotype/sleep outcomes using structural equation modeling (reported in abstract). t-tests and SEM used to assess predictors of chronotype, social jet lag, and weekday sleep duration.

FT_IC4.evidence: Cohen's d effect sizes reported: screen time on weekday evenings (d=0.27), weekend evenings (d=0.35). SEM model explained variance percentages reported: 12.9% for chronotype, 10.5% for social jet lag, 6.2% for weekday sleep duration.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using ABCD Study data with t-tests and structural equation modeling examining relationships between caffeine intake, screen time, and sleep outcomes.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published in Sleep Advances: a journal of the Sleep Research Society, 2025. Journal article, not a preprint or duplicate.

C2.FT_EC7_reason: Screen time is an independent variable (predictor), not the dependent variable. Paper examines associations between screen time and chronotype/sleep outcomes. Does not meet EC7 criteria (EC7 only applies when screen time/SM is the DV with demographic-only longitudinal predictors).

title: What we know about screen time and social media in early adolescence: a review of findings from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study.
abstract: PURPOSE OF REVIEW: To review recent literature based on Adolescent Brain and Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study data of over 11â000 participants about screen time and social media use in early adolescence, including epidemiology, trends, and associations with mental and physical health outcomes. RECENT FINDINGS: Time spent on screens and social media has risen among early adolescents (aged 9-15âyears), and these increasing trends have been more pronounced since the COVID-19 pandemic. Both cross-sectional and 1-2-year follow-up data show that higher screen use, particularly social media, video games, video chat, videos, and texting, is associated with a range of mental health symptoms, including depression, anxiety, eating disorders, obsessive-compulsive disorder, attention-deficit/hyperactivity, and disruptive behavior disorders, although effect sizes vary. Screen time is also associated with sleep problems and various cardiometabolic risk factors. Parents' own screen use and restriction of bedroom and mealtime screen use are associated with lower early adolescent screen time and problematic use. SUMMARY: Analyses of current ABCD Study data reveal increasing use of screens and social media across early adolescence, with sociodemographic disparities in screen use as well as numerous associations between screens and adverse mental and physical health effects.

FT_IC1.evidence: Review of ABCD Study data with over 11,000 participants; explicitly states it reviews findings from ABCD Study

FT_IC2.evidence: Discusses specific smartphone/social media variables: screen time, social media use, video games, video chat, texting

FT_IC3.evidence: No PDF available; abstract indicates review of associations but does not specify whether this is an original analysis with inferential statistics or a literature review without primary analysis

FT_IC4.evidence: No PDF available; abstract does not provide numeric coefficients or statistics

FT_IC5.evidence: No PDF available; title indicates 'review of findings' suggesting it may be a review article rather than original empirical analysis

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article (Current Opinion in Pediatrics, 2025) with DOI 10.1097/mop.0000000000001462

C1.FT_EC7_reason: Cannot determine FT-EC7 applicability without PDF. If article is a review, it would not report original analyses and would be excluded on FT-IC3/FT-IC5 grounds before FT-EC7 evaluation.

FT_IC1.evidence: Review of Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study data of over 11,000 participants

FT_IC2.evidence: Reviews associations with specific SM variables: social media, video games, video chat, videos, texting

FT_IC3.evidence: Reviews cross-sectional and 1-2-year follow-up data showing associations between screen use and mental health outcomes

FT_IC4.evidence: No PDF available; abstract only. Cannot verify numeric coefficients reported

FT_IC5.evidence: This is a review article summarizing findings from ABCD Study, not original empirical analysis. No original analyses conducted by authors

FT_IC6.evidence: Published in Current Opinion in Pediatrics, 2025

C2.FT_EC7_reason: Not applicable; FT-IC5 failed (review article, not original empirical work)
